content:
# Philippians Chapter 2 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Philippians Chapter 2 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) diverged in their English renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## Verse 1

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "If therefore [there is] any encouragement in Christ, if any consolation of love, if any fellowship of [the] Spirit, if any compassions and mercies" |
| LOGOS | "If therefore there is any encouragement in Christ, if any consolation of love, if any fellowship of the Spirit, if any affection and compassion" |
| PNEUMA | "So if being united with Christ means anything to you — if his love brings you any comfort, if the Spirit draws you into real fellowship, if you have any tenderness or compassion at all —" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The final pair (splanchna kai oiktirmoi) is rendered "affection and compassion" — "compassions and mercies" (EMET) is too archaic; PNEUMA's "tenderness or compassion" is accurate but the restructured conditional clauses depart too far from Paul's fourfold parallel "if...if...if...if" construction. LOGOS preserves both the rhetorical structure and natural English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 2

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "fill-full my joy, that the same thing you-might-think, having the same love, joined-in-soul, the one thing thinking" |
| LOGOS | "fulfill my joy, that you be of the same mind, having the same love, united in soul, thinking the one thing" |
| PNEUMA | "then make my joy complete: be of one mind, share the same love, be united in soul and purpose" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Fulfill my joy" is both accurate and natural. "United in soul" (LOGOS) and "joined-in-soul" (EMET) both render sympsychoi; PNEUMA's addition of "and purpose" is interpretive. "Thinking the one thing" preserves the emphatic Greek to hen phronountes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 3

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "nothing according-to selfish-ambition nor according-to vainglory, but in the lowliness-of-mind one-another considering-as-surpassing yourselves" |
| LOGOS | "Do nothing according to selfish ambition or according to vainglory, but in humility of mind let each regard the others as surpassing themselves" |
| PNEUMA | "Never act out of selfish ambition or empty pride. Instead, with genuine humility, count others as more important than yourselves." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Humility of mind" renders tapeinophrosyne accurately. PNEUMA's "empty pride" for kenodoxia is a valid dynamic rendering but "vainglory" preserves the compound Greek form (kenos + doxa = empty glory). EMET's hyphenated compounds are too stiff for a consensus text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 4

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "not the [things] of yourselves each looking-at, but also the [things] of others each [looking-at]" |
| LOGOS | "each of you looking not to your own interests, but also each of you to the interests of others" |
| PNEUMA | "Do not look out only for your own interests, but pay attention to the interests of others as well" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. All three convey the same sense. LOGOS supplies "interests" (implicit in the Greek ta heauton) while preserving the "each" (hekastoi — notably plural in Sinaiticus). PNEUMA's "pay attention to" is natural but loses the structural parallelism.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 5

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For this think in you which also in Christ Jesus" |
| LOGOS | "Have this mind among yourselves which was also in Christ Jesus" |
| PNEUMA | "Let the same mindset be in you that was in Christ Jesus:" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek touto phroneite en hymin ho kai en Christo Iesou has no explicit verb in the relative clause. LOGOS supplies "was" (indicative reading), which PNEUMA also follows. EMET leaves the verb unsupplied, which preserves the Greek ambiguity (indicative vs. imperative) but is unreadable in English. LOGOS's "Have this mind among yourselves" captures the imperative phroneite clearly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's note on the missing verb is valuable for study.

---

## Verse 6

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "who in [the] form of God existing, not robbery considered the being equal with God" |
| LOGOS | "who, being in the form of God, did not consider equality with God something to be grasped" |
| PNEUMA | "Who, though he existed in the very form of God, did not regard equality with God as something to be seized and exploited." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. This is the most theologically contested word in the chapter: harpagmon. EMET's "robbery" preserves the abstract noun but is misleading in English. PNEUMA's "seized and exploited" is interpretively rich but adds "exploited" beyond the Greek. LOGOS's "something to be grasped" preserves the ambiguity between res rapta ("a thing already grasped") and res rapienda ("a thing to be grasped at"), which is exactly the scholarly crux. The concessive participle hyparchon is rendered "being" (LOGOS) rather than "though he existed" (PNEUMA), which adds a concessive nuance not explicit in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. This is the single most consequential translation decision in the chapter.

---

## Verse 7

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "but himself he-emptied, [the] form of a slave having-taken, in [the] likeness of men having-become; and in outward-appearance having-been-found as a man" |
| LOGOS | "but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being made in the likeness of men." |
| PNEUMA | "Instead, he emptied himself completely, taking on the form of a slave, being born in human likeness." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS with one change: "slave" instead of "servant." EMET and PNEUMA both render doulos as "slave," which is the correct denotation — a doulos has no rights or autonomy. The contrast with morphe theou ("form of God") in v. 6 demands the strongest possible term. LOGOS's "being made in the likeness of men" is preferred over PNEUMA's "being born in human likeness" because genomenos means "having become," not specifically "born." PNEUMA's "completely" is not in the Greek. SOPHIA renders: "but emptied himself, taking the form of a slave, being made in the likeness of men."

**Status:** Resolved as hybrid: LOGOS structure with EMET/PNEUMA's "slave" for doulos.

---

## Verse 8

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he-humbled himself, having-become obedient unto death, and death of a cross" |
| LOGOS | "And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself, becoming obedient unto death — even death on a cross." |
| PNEUMA | "And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself even further, becoming obedient all the way to death — yes, even death on a cross." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Note that EMET attaches "being found in appearance as a man" to the end of v. 7, while LOGOS and PNEUMA place it at the beginning of v. 8; SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA here, which better parallels the hymnic structure. PNEUMA adds "even further" and "all the way to" — these intensifiers are not in the Greek. The em-dash before "even death on a cross" (LOGOS) captures the dramatic force of the Greek de, which marks Paul's own intensifying addition to the hymn.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 9

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Therefore also God him highly-exalted and granted to him the name the [one] above every name" |
| LOGOS | "Therefore God also highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name" |
| PNEUMA | "And so God raised him to the highest place and gave him the name that stands above every name" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Highly exalted" renders hyperypsosen, the compound verb with hyper- prefix. PNEUMA's "raised him to the highest place" is interpretive paraphrase. "Bestowed" (LOGOS) for echarisato is more precise than "granted" (EMET) or "gave" (PNEUMA), as the Greek root is charis (grace) — the name is a gracious gift.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 10

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "that in the name of Jesus every knee should-bow, of [those in] heaven and of [those] on earth and of [those] under-the-earth" |
| LOGOS | "that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, of those in heaven and those on earth and those under the earth" |
| PNEUMA | "so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow — in heaven, on earth, and under the earth —" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The preposition en to onomati is rendered "at the name" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) rather than "in the name" (EMET). "At" better conveys the response triggered by the name being spoken. PNEUMA's em-dashes and simplified phrasing lose the genitive construction (epouranion, epigeion, katachthonion) which describes categories of beings, not merely locations.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 11

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and every tongue should-confess that Lord [is] Jesus Christ, to [the] glory of God [the] Father" |
| LOGOS | "and every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father" |
| PNEUMA | "and every tongue declare: 'Jesus Christ is Lord!' to the glory of God the Father." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The word order differs: EMET follows the Greek word order (Kyrios Iesous Christos — "Lord Jesus Christ") where Kyrios is the predicate placed first for emphasis. LOGOS rearranges to natural English predication ("Jesus Christ is Lord"). PNEUMA's "declare" for exomologesetai is less precise than "confess," which carries the sense of open, public acknowledgment. The quotation marks in PNEUMA are not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The confession "Jesus Christ is Lord" is the climactic theological declaration of the hymn.

---

## Verse 12

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "So-then, my beloved-ones, just-as you-always obeyed, not as in my presence only, but now much more in my absence, with fear and trembling your-own salvation work-out" |
| LOGOS | "Therefore, my beloved, just as you have always obeyed, not as in my presence only but now much more in my absence, work out your own salvation with fear and trembling" |
| PNEUMA | "And so, my dear friends — you who have always obeyed, not only when I was present but even more now in my absence — keep working out your salvation with reverence and trembling." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Beloved" for agapetoi is more precise than PNEUMA's "dear friends." PNEUMA's "reverence" for phobos is interpretive — the Greek means "fear." PNEUMA's "keep working out" implies continuous action (valid from the present imperative) but adds nuance not all readers will find in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 13

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for God is the [one] working in you both the willing and the working for the [sake of his] good-pleasure" |
| LOGOS | "for it is God who is at work in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure" |
| PNEUMA | "For it is God himself who is at work in you, both to ignite your desire and to fuel your action, according to his good purpose." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "ignite your desire and fuel your action" is vivid but too interpretive. "To will and to work" renders to thelein kai to energein with appropriate restraint. PNEUMA's "good purpose" for eudokia is acceptable but "good pleasure" better preserves the warmth of the Greek, which connotes delight, not merely intention.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 14

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "All-things do without grumblings and disputes" |
| LOGOS | "Do all things without grumbling and without disputing" |
| PNEUMA | "Do everything without grumbling or arguing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a hybrid: "Do all things without grumbling and disputing." LOGOS's double "without" is slightly redundant. PNEUMA's "arguing" for dialogismos is too colloquial; the Greek suggests internal reasoning/questioning rather than overt argument. "Disputing" (LOGOS) is more accurate.

**Status:** Resolved as hybrid.

---

## Verse 15

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "that you-might-become blameless and unmixed, children of God unblemished in [the] midst of a crooked and having-been-perverted generation, among whom you-shine as luminaries in [the] world" |
| LOGOS | "that you may become blameless and innocent, children of God without blemish in the midst of a crooked and perverted generation, among whom you shine as lights in the world" |
| PNEUMA | "so that you may become blameless and pure, children of God without fault in a crooked and twisted generation, among whom you shine like stars blazing in the night sky" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EMET's "unmixed" for akeraios is the literal etymology (a-kerannymi) but "innocent" is the established meaning in context. PNEUMA's "pure" is close but less precise. "Without blemish" (LOGOS) is more formal than PNEUMA's "without fault." "Lights in the world" (LOGOS) preserves the Greek phosteres en kosmo without PNEUMA's expansive "stars blazing in the night sky," which, while evocative, adds imagery not present in the text. "Perverted" (LOGOS) for diestrammenes is closer to the Greek than PNEUMA's "twisted."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 16

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] word of life holding-forth, for a boast to me for [the] day of Christ, that not in vain I-ran nor in vain I-labored" |
| LOGOS | "holding fast the word of life, so that in the day of Christ I may have cause to boast that I did not run in vain nor labor in vain" |
| PNEUMA | "holding out the word of life. Then on the day of Christ I will have reason to boast that I did not run in vain or labor for nothing." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The verb epechontes can mean either "holding fast" (intransitive) or "holding forth/out" (transitive). LOGOS reads "holding fast," PNEUMA reads "holding out." Both are defensible; SOPHIA follows LOGOS, as the intransitive sense better fits the context of perseverance. "Labor in vain" (LOGOS) is preferred over PNEUMA's "labor for nothing" — the Greek eis kenon is literally "into emptiness."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 17

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But if even I-am-being-poured-out-as-a-drink-offering upon the sacrifice and priestly-service of your faith, I-rejoice and I-rejoice-together with all of you" |
| LOGOS | "But even if I am being poured out as a drink offering upon the sacrifice and service of your faith, I rejoice and share my joy with you all." |
| PNEUMA | "But even if my life is poured out like a drink offering on the sacrifice and service that your faith produces, I rejoice — and I share that joy with all of you." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EMET's "priestly-service" for leitourgia is etymologically precise but overly technical. LOGOS's "service" is adequate. PNEUMA adds "my life" and "that your faith produces" — interpretive additions. "Share my joy with you all" (LOGOS) renders synchairō naturally.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 18

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and the same also you rejoice and rejoice-together with me" |
| LOGOS | "And in the same way you also rejoice and share your joy with me." |
| PNEUMA | "In the same way, you too should rejoice, and share your joy with me." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "should," making the indicative into an imperative. The Greek chairete kai synchairete moi can be either indicative or imperative, but the parallel with v. 17 (where Paul states his own rejoicing as fact) favors the indicative reading.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 19

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But I-hope in [the] Lord Jesus Timothy to-send quickly to you, that I-also might-be-of-good-spirit having-known the [things] concerning you" |
| LOGOS | "But I hope in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so that I also may be encouraged when I know of your circumstances." |
| PNEUMA | "I trust in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so that I too may be encouraged when I learn how things are with you." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS with minor adjustment: "learn" from PNEUMA replaces "know" for gnous (aorist participle), since "learn" better captures the ingressive sense of coming to know. EMET's "might-be-of-good-spirit" for eupsycho is literal but awkward; "be encouraged" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) conveys the meaning. PNEUMA's "trust" for elpizo is stronger than the Greek warrants — "hope" is standard.

**Status:** Resolved as hybrid (LOGOS + PNEUMA's "learn").

---

## Verse 20

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For no-one I-have like-souled, who genuinely the [things] concerning you will-care-for" |
| LOGOS | "For I have no one of like mind who will genuinely be anxious for your circumstances." |
| PNEUMA | "I have no one else like him — someone who will genuinely care about your welfare." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS with adjustment: "care for" replaces "be anxious for." The Greek merimnesei can mean both "be anxious about" and "care for." In context, Paul commends Timothy's concern as a virtue, so "care for" (closer to PNEUMA) avoids the negative connotations of anxiety. "Like mind" (LOGOS) renders isopsychon well. PNEUMA's "no one else like him" loses the specific Greek term.

**Status:** Resolved as hybrid (LOGOS + "care for" from PNEUMA).

---

## Verse 21

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "for the all the [things] of themselves they-seek, not the [things] of Christ Jesus" |
| LOGOS | "For they all seek their own interests, not those of Christ Jesus." |
| PNEUMA | "For everyone else is looking out for their own interests, not those of Jesus Christ." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "everyone else" and "looking out for" are natural but loose. EMET notes the article (hoi pantes, "the all") — a sweeping indictment. LOGOS captures this with "they all." The name order "Christ Jesus" follows the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 22

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But the proven-character of him you-know, that as a father [with] a child, with me he-served-as-a-slave for the gospel." |
| LOGOS | "But you know his proven character, that as a child with a father he served with me in the gospel." |
| PNEUMA | "But you know his proven character: like a son serving alongside his father, he has worked with me in the cause of the gospel." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Note: EMET reverses the analogy ("as a father with a child") while LOGOS and PNEUMA correctly render "as a child with a father" — Timothy is the child, Paul the father. The Greek reads hos patri teknon, "as to a father a child [serves]." PNEUMA's "in the cause of" adds interpretive weight. LOGOS's "in the gospel" is sufficient.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's reversal of the father-child analogy is a translation error.

---

## Verse 23

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "This-one therefore I-hope to-send, as soon-as I-might-see-clearly the [things] concerning me, immediately" |
| LOGOS | "Him therefore I hope to send at once, as soon as I see how things go with me." |
| PNEUMA | "So I hope to send him as soon as I can see how things turn out for me." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek exautes means "at once/immediately" — LOGOS places it clearly ("at once"). EMET's "immediately" at the end is awkward. PNEUMA omits it. "How things go with me" (LOGOS) renders ta peri eme naturally.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 24

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "but I-have-confidence in [the] Lord that I myself also quickly will-come" |
| LOGOS | "But I am confident in the Lord that I myself also shall come soon." |
| PNEUMA | "And I am confident in the Lord that I myself will come to you before long." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Shall come soon" is slightly more formal and precise than PNEUMA's "will come to you before long." PNEUMA adds "to you," which is implied but not stated in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 25

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "But necessary I-considered Epaphroditus, the brother and fellow-worker and fellow-soldier of me, but your apostle and minister of my need, to-send to you" |
| LOGOS | "But I considered it necessary to send to you Epaphroditus, my brother and fellow worker and fellow soldier, and your messenger and minister to my need" |
| PNEUMA | "But for now I thought it necessary to send Epaphroditus back to you — my brother, my co-worker, my fellow soldier, your messenger and minister to my need —" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EMET retains "apostle" for apostolon, but LOGOS's "messenger" correctly renders the non-technical usage here (Epaphroditus is the Philippians' envoy, not an Apostle). PNEUMA adds "back" and "for now" — interpretive additions. LOGOS preserves the chain of five titles without embellishment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 26

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "since-indeed he-was longing-for you all, and being-distressed because you-heard that he-was-sick" |
| LOGOS | "since he was longing for you all and was distressed because you had heard that he was ill" |
| PNEUMA | "because he has been longing for all of you and was deeply distressed that you heard he was ill" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "deeply" before "distressed" — ademoneo already carries strong emotional weight without intensification. "Ill" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is preferred over EMET's "sick" for smoothness. LOGOS's "had heard" preserves the temporal sequence.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 27

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For indeed he-was-sick, close to death; but God had-mercy-on him, and not him only, but also me, that not sorrow upon sorrow I-might-have." |
| LOGOS | "For indeed he was ill, near to death; but God had mercy on him, and not on him only but also on me, so that I might not have sorrow upon sorrow." |
| PNEUMA | "And indeed he was ill — dangerously close to death. But God had mercy on him, and not only on him but also on me, sparing me sorrow upon sorrow." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "dangerously close" adds an adverb not in the Greek (paraplēsion thanatō = "near to death"). PNEUMA's "sparing me" restructures the purpose clause. LOGOS preserves the Greek syntax faithfully.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 28

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "More-eagerly therefore I-sent him, that having-seen him again you-might-rejoice, and I might-be less-sorrowful." |
| LOGOS | "Therefore I have sent him all the more eagerly, so that when you see him again you may rejoice and I may be less anxious." |
| PNEUMA | "So I am all the more eager to send him, so that when you see him again you may rejoice, and my own anxiety may be eased." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EMET's "less-sorrowful" for alypoteros is etymologically precise (a-lypos = without grief), but "less anxious" (LOGOS) fits the context of Paul's concern. PNEUMA restructures to "my own anxiety may be eased," which loses the comparative adjective. The epistolary aorist epempsa is rendered "I have sent" (LOGOS) rather than present tense (PNEUMA).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 29

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Receive therefore him in [the] Lord with all joy, and the such-ones hold-in-honor" |
| LOGOS | "Receive him therefore in the Lord with all joy, and hold such men in honor" |
| PNEUMA | "Welcome him in the Lord with great joy, and hold people like him in the highest honor" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "welcome" for prosdechesthe is warmer but "receive" is more precise. "Great joy" (PNEUMA) interprets pases charas ("all joy") loosely. PNEUMA's "highest honor" adds a superlative not in the Greek (entimous = "honored, precious"). "Hold such men in honor" (LOGOS) is accurate.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Verse 30

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "because on-account-of the work of Christ unto death he-drew-near, having-risked the life, that he-might-fill-up your lack of the toward me priestly-service" |
| LOGOS | "because for the work of Christ he came near to death, having risked his life, in order to complete what was lacking in your service to me" |
| PNEUMA | "because he nearly died for the work of Christ, risking his very life to make up for the help you could not give me in person" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "help you could not give me in person" interprets the abstract leitourgias ("service/ministry") too freely. LOGOS's "to complete what was lacking in your service to me" preserves the Greek anaplērosē to hymon hysterēma tēs pros me leitourgias with precision. EMET's "priestly-service" overloads leitourgia with cultic meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

*All 30 verses of Philippians 2 showed divergence among the three translators and are logged above.*

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
